section.title: The Divisions of the Priests
section.sectionRationale: Having organized the Levites in chapter 23, the Chronicler now turns to the priests themselves, the narrower family of Aaron. Nadab and Abihu died childless for their unauthorized fire, so only Eleazar's and Ithamar's lines remain to serve. David, with Zadok and Ahimelech, divides them by lot into twenty-four courses, then does the same for the remaining Levitical families. It is an unglamorous chapter of names and numbers, but it fixes forever the rotation that governed access to God's altar.
section.thesis: David orders the thinned and scattered house of Aaron into twenty-four courses chosen by lot, so that the priesthood, purified by past judgment and organized by God's own means of decision, could serve the LORD in a fair and settled rotation, a structure the returning exiles would reestablish and that still stood centuries later when the division of Abijah brought Zechariah into the sanctuary, Luke 1:5, on the eve of the gospel.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the line of Aaron reduced to two houses and set in order under David, Zadok, and Ahimelech; the lot cast for the twenty-four priestly courses one by one; and the rest of the Levitical families likewise numbered and given their lot before the king and the assembly.

units.label: The line of Aaron reduced and ordered
units.note: Nadab and Abihu died; Eleazar and Ithamar remain

verses.text: These were the divisions of the descendants of Aaron. The sons of Aaron were Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
But Nadab and Abihu died before their father did, and they had no sons; so Eleazar and Ithamar served as priests.
With the help of Eleazar's descendant Zadok and Ithamar's descendant Ahimelech, David divided them according to the offices of their service.

Since more leaders were found among Eleazar's descendants than those of Ithamar, they were divided accordingly. There were sixteen heads of families from the descendants of Eleazar and eight from the descendants of Ithamar.
Thus they were divided by {{goral|lot}}, for there were officers of the sanctuary and officers of God among both Eleazar's and Ithamar's descendants.

The scribe, Shemaiah son of Nethanel, a Levite, recorded their names in the presence of the king and of the officers: Zadok the {{kohen|priest}}, Ahimelech son of Abiathar, and the heads of families of the priests and the Levites, one family being taken from Eleazar, and then one from Ithamar.
The first lot fell to Jehoiarib, the second to Jedaiah,
the third to Harim, the fourth to Seorim,
the fifth to Malchijah, the sixth to Mijamin,
the seventh to Hakkoz, the eighth to Abijah,
the ninth to Jeshua, the tenth to Shecaniah,
the eleventh to Eliashib, the twelfth to Jakim,
the thirteenth to Huppah, the fourteenth to Jeshebeab,
the fifteenth to Bilgah, the sixteenth to Immer,
the seventeenth to Hezir, the eighteenth to Happizzez,
the nineteenth to Pethahiah, the twentieth to Jehezkel,
the twenty-first to Jachin, the twenty-second to Gamul,
the twenty-third to Delaiah, and the twenty-fourth to Maaziah.

This was their appointed order for service when they entered the house of the LORD, according to the regulations prescribed for them by their forefather Aaron, as the LORD, the God of Israel, had commanded him.

units.label: The rest of the Levites, also numbered and set
units.note: the same fair lot for every family

verses.text: Now these were the rest of the descendants of Levi: From the sons of Amram: Shubael; from the sons of Shubael: Jehdeiah.
As for Rehabiah, from his sons: The first was Isshiah.
From the Izharites: Shelomoth; from the sons of Shelomoth: Jahath.
From the sons of Hebron: Jeriah was the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, and Jekameam the fourth.
From the sons of Uzziel: Micah; from the sons of Micah: Shamir.
The brother of Micah: Isshiah; from the sons of Isshiah: Zechariah.
The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. The son of Jaaziah: Beno.
The descendants of Merari from Jaaziah: Beno, Shoham, Zaccur, and Ibri.
From Mahli: Eleazar, who had no sons.
From Kish: Jerahmeel the son of Kish.
And the sons of Mushi: Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth. These were the sons of the Levites, according to their families.

As their brothers the descendants of Aaron did, they also cast lots in the presence of King David and of Zadok, Ahimelech, and the heads of the families of the priests and Levites, the family heads and their younger brothers alike.

goral.gloss: Lot, an object cast to reach a decision understood to belong finally to the LORD, Proverbs 16:33. Priestly and Levitical service, gatekeeping, land, and even Saul's exposure all turn on the goral in Scripture; here it settles the order of twenty-four priestly courses without favoritism or human politics.

kohen.gloss: Priest, one set apart from the tribe of Levi and the house of Aaron to minister at the altar and in the sanctuary. Only Eleazar's and Ithamar's lines remain kohanim after Nadab and Abihu's death, and it is their descendants, Zadok and Ahimelech, who oversee this division of the whole priesthood.

basic.context: By this point in David's reign, the Levites have already been counted and organized in chapter 23. Now the Chronicler zooms in on the narrower priestly family within Levi, the sons of Aaron. Two of Aaron's four sons, Nadab and Abihu, had died long before this for offering unauthorized fire, Leviticus 10:1-2, and left no sons, so all priestly service now runs through Eleazar and Ithamar. David, working with Zadok, from Eleazar's line, and Ahimelech, from Ithamar's line, divides these families into twenty-four courses by casting lots, so that every family, whether large or small in number, shares equally in the honor and burden of serving in God's house on a rotating basis. The chapter then briefly lists the rest of the Levitical families and notes that they too cast lots in exactly the same way, before the king and the whole leadership.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical, administrative narrative, not a command for the church to reproduce sacred lots or fixed rotas. It describes how Israel's worship was ordered under the old covenant. What it teaches for every reader is that God cares about order, fairness, and the practical, unglamorous work of arranging His people's service, and that no family's privilege or long pedigree earns them a bigger share than the impartial lot allowed. It quietly answers a painful memory too, that even after the judgment on Nadab and Abihu, God still had a priesthood and a plan.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on the goral, the lot, cast to settle each of the twenty-four courses without partiality, since Proverbs 16:33 says the lot is cast but its every decision is from the LORD. And it is the kohen, the priest, the title clinging to only two surviving sons of Aaron, Eleazar and Ithamar, whose descendants Zadok and Ahimelech now organize a priesthood that will still be running when Luke opens his Gospel with the division of Abijah, the eighth course cast here in verse 10.
basic.typology: The two surviving priestly lines, thinned by judgment yet preserved and multiplied by grace, foreshadow a greater pattern: God does not abandon the priesthood to failure but keeps and grows a people to serve Him, until He raises up a final Priest who never dies and never fails, Hebrews 7:23-24. The fair lot, belonging finally to the LORD, whispers of a King who will one day order all things justly and without partiality.
basic.christ: Zechariah, a priest of the division of Abijah, the very course established by this chapter, was serving his appointed week in the sanctuary when the angel announced the coming of John the Baptist, Luke 1:5-13, the forerunner of Jesus. The everyday, centuries-old order fixed in 1 Chronicles 24 is still turning when heaven finally breaks in to announce the Messiah. And where this priesthood was many, rotating, dying, and needing constant renewal, the writer to the Hebrews shows that Jesus is the priest who holds His priesthood permanently, because He continues forever, Hebrews 7:23-25, able to save completely those who draw near to God through Him.
basic.god: God is a God of order, not chaos, arranging even the rotation of priestly service down to families and names. He is also a God who disciplines sin, Nadab and Abihu's unauthorized fire cost them everything, yet He does not let judgment be the last word; He preserves a priesthood through Eleazar and Ithamar and multiplies it richly. And He is impartial: the lot, not human preference or seniority, decides each family's place, so that even the humblest priestly house stands equal before Him.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 10:1-2 records the judgment on Nadab and Abihu behind this chapter. Proverbs 16:33 explains the theology of the lot. Luke 1:5-13 shows the division of Abijah, established in verse 10, still governing temple service when Gabriel appears to Zechariah. Hebrews 7:23-25 contrasts this many, mortal priesthood with the permanent priesthood of Christ.
basic.oneStory: The house of Aaron, cut down by judgment yet preserved by grace, is fairly and carefully ordered so that worship at God's house can continue, generation after generation, all the way to the priest of Abijah's course who was serving when the angel announced that the Messiah's herald was coming. The whole scene points toward Jesus, our great high priest, whose priesthood never rotates off duty and never needs replacing.

context.literary: This chapter continues the long block of chapters 23 to 27 that organizes Levites, priests, musicians, gatekeepers, and officials, all preparation for Solomon's temple that the Chronicler places right after David hands over the building plans in chapter 22. The dense list of names is broken by two clear structuring notes, verses 5 and 31, both underlining that the lot was cast fairly for every family, great or small.
context.historical: By the time the Chronicler wrote, centuries after David, the twenty-four courses established here still structured the priesthood's rotating service, two weeks per course roughly twice a year plus the pilgrim feasts, a structure known from later Jewish sources and confirmed by Luke's casual mention of Zechariah's course. For the returned exiles rebuilding worship after Babylon, knowing exactly which families belonged to which ancient course, fixed by David himself, gave stability and legitimacy to a fragile, rebuilding priesthood.
context.canonical: This chapter sits within Scripture's larger concern for a faithful priesthood, running from Aaron's original ordination, Exodus 28-29, through the judgment on Nadab and Abihu, the zeal of Phinehas, and eventually to the New Testament's declaration that Jesus is a priest after the order of Melchizedek, superior to the Aaronic order altogether, Hebrews 7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record how David, cooperating with Zadok and Ahimelech, organized the reduced house of Aaron and the rest of the Levites into a fair, lot-determined order of service, giving the post-exilic priesthood a legitimate and traceable structure to restore.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific administrative arrangement for old covenant temple service; it does not prescribe casting lots or maintaining rotating courses for the church today. What is abidingly true is God's care for order, fairness between families of unequal size or status, and His faithfulness to preserve a priesthood even after severe judgment.

debates.question: Why does the text stress that Eleazar had more leaders than Ithamar, verse 4?

views.label: A simple demographic fact requiring adjusted lots
views.case: The text plainly says more family heads arose from Eleazar's line, so the sixteen-to-eight split of courses reflects that reality; the lot then decided which specific families received which of the twenty-four slots, preserving fairness within an unequal starting number.

views.label: A reminder that Eleazar was always the appointed line
views.case: Eleazar had already been marked as Aaron's true priestly successor after Nadab and Abihu died, Numbers 20:25-28, so the imbalance in family heads is also a quiet confirmation that the chief priestly line, culminating in Zadok, rightly carries the larger share of the courses.

debates.bearing: Either way, the lot itself, not human preference, decides which specific families of each line hold which of the twenty-four numbered courses, so no family could claim favoritism in the final assignment.

debates.question: Why record such a long, apparently mundane list of names at all?

views.label: To give returning exiles a verifiable, legitimate priesthood
views.case: After Babylon, priestly lineage had to be provable, Ezra 2:61-63 shows men excluded from the priesthood for lacking genealogical proof, so a fixed, named, court-witnessed record like this one gave returning families a document to appeal to when reestablishing who could serve.

views.label: To show that ordinary, administrative faithfulness is itself an act of worship
views.case: The Chronicler consistently treats organizational detail, numbers, names, and rotas as worthy of inspired record because arranging God's house rightly, however unglamorous, is itself an act of obedience and worship, not a lesser task beneath the more exciting story of battles and kings.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold together: the list is theological as much as administrative, teaching that God's people honor Him by getting the unglamorous details of worship right and by preserving the truthful record that lets others verify and continue that worship.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's backbone is the goral, the lot, cast twice over, once for the twenty-four priestly courses and again for the remaining Levitical families, verses 5 and 31, each time stressed as fair to great and small alike; Proverbs 16:33 explains why this mattered so much, the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD. And the whole chapter exists because of the kohen, the priest: two of Aaron's four sons bore that title into extinction through unauthorized worship, while Eleazar's and Ithamar's descendants carried it onward, all the way to a nameless-to-us priest of the eighth course, Abijah, whose day of service in the sanctuary, still running centuries later, became the setting for Gabriel's announcement in Luke 1.

typology.type: The priesthood cut down by judgment yet preserved through grace
typology.fulfillment: God's pattern of disciplining sin without abandoning His purposes, ultimately climaxing in a priesthood, Christ's, that can never be cut off, Hebrews 7:23-25.

typology.type: The impartial lot that gives every family its place
typology.fulfillment: The justice of God, who shows no favoritism, echoed in the gospel's welcome to Jew and Gentile alike on equal footing, Acts 10:34-35.

typology.type: The rotating, temporary courses of Aaron's sons
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the permanent priest whose priesthood does not pass from one man to another, Hebrews 7:24.

study.christ: The eighth course established in this chapter, Abijah, verse 10, is still governing temple service centuries later when Zechariah, a priest of that division, is serving his appointed time and is told by Gabriel that his son will go before the Lord to prepare His way, Luke 1:5-17. The everyday machinery this chapter sets up quietly carries God's purposes all the way to the doorstep of the incarnation. And the whole chapter's underlying need, priests who die, whose courses must rotate and be renewed generation after generation, is answered in Jesus, who holds His priesthood permanently because He continues forever, and who is therefore able to save completely those who draw near to God through Him, Hebrews 7:23-25.
study.god: God is orderly, fair, and faithful. He arranges the rotation of His house down to the level of families and names, refusing to leave worship to chance or favoritism even while using the lot, which Scripture says belongs finally to Him, to settle every assignment. He is also the God who disciplines sin severely, as with Nadab and Abihu, yet who does not let that judgment be His last word for the priesthood; He preserves, multiplies, and organizes a full company of priests through the surviving lines. His faithfulness reaches across centuries, so that the very structure fixed here in David's day is still standing, unbroken, when He sends His Son.

crossReferences.note: Nadab and Abihu's offering of unauthorized fire and their deaths, which stand behind the reduced priestly line in this chapter.
Eleazar is invested as Aaron's priestly successor, explaining the prominence of his line here.
The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, the theology behind the goral in this chapter.
Zechariah, of the division of Abijah established in verse 10, is serving in the sanctuary when Gabriel announces the forerunner of Christ.
Contrasts the many, mortal priests of the rotating courses with Jesus, who holds His priesthood permanently.

study.oneStory: The house of Aaron, thinned by judgment yet kept alive by grace, is here fairly and carefully ordered into twenty-four courses so that worship at God's house could continue without interruption or favoritism, generation after generation. That same unglamorous structure was still turning centuries later when a priest of the eighth course, Abijah, was serving his appointed week and was told by an angel that the Messiah's herald was coming. From the fall of Nadab and Abihu to the courts of Herod's temple, God was quietly, faithfully preserving a priesthood for the day He would send the great High Priest who needs no rotation and no replacement, Jesus Christ.

quiz.q: Why did priestly service run only through Eleazar and Ithamar rather than all four of Aaron's sons?

options.text: Nadab and Abihu died childless after offering unauthorized fire before the LORD, leaving only Eleazar's and Ithamar's lines.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explain that Nadab and Abihu died before their father and left no sons.

options.text: Nadab and Abihu simply chose not to serve as priests.
options.note: It was death for unauthorized fire, not a personal choice to step aside, Leviticus 10:1-2.

options.text: Aaron only ever had two sons.
options.note: Verse 1 names all four of Aaron's sons; two of them died without heirs.

options.text: Eleazar and Ithamar were older and so inherited the role automatically.
options.note: Birth order is not the stated reason; the text specifically credits their deaths without sons, not seniority.

quiz.q: How did David divide the priestly families into their courses of service?

options.text: By casting lots, so that the assignment of each of the twenty-four courses came from the LORD, not human preference.
options.note: Verse 5 states plainly that they were divided by lot, for officers of the sanctuary were found among both lines.

options.text: By seniority of birth among the family heads.
options.note: The number of heads from each line affected how many courses each line received, but the specific assignment came by lot, not birth order alone.

options.text: By David's personal preference for certain families.
options.note: The text stresses the impartial lot, not royal favoritism.

options.text: By counting how many descendants each family had and ranking them.
options.note: Family size shaped the sixteen-to-eight split between Eleazar's and Ithamar's lines, but the final course assignments still came by lot.

quiz.q: How many courses of priestly service did David establish, and why does this detail matter later in Scripture?

options.text: Twenty-four courses, one of which, Abijah, was still governing temple service when Zechariah received Gabriel's announcement in Luke 1.
options.note: Verses 7-18 list all twenty-four lots; Luke 1:5 places Zechariah in the division of Abijah, the eighth course named in verse 10.

options.text: Twelve courses, matching the twelve tribes of Israel.
options.note: The text lists twenty-four courses, not twelve, matching neither tribal count nor months alone.

options.text: Twenty-four courses, though the arrangement was abandoned before the exile.
options.note: Far from being abandoned, the structure endured for centuries, still functioning in Luke's Gospel.

options.text: Twenty-four courses, primarily useful for organizing temple music.
options.note: The courses in this chapter organize priestly altar service; musicians receive their own twenty-four courses in the following chapter.

quiz.q: What happened when the rest of the Levitical families, beyond the priests, were organized?

options.text: They too cast lots in the presence of the king and the leaders, family heads and younger brothers treated alike.
options.note: Verse 31 says they cast lots just as the descendants of Aaron did, with no favoritism between older and younger.

options.text: They were exempted from the lot and simply assigned by David's decree.
options.note: Verse 31 specifically says they cast lots as their brothers, the priests, had done.

options.text: They were given fewer responsibilities since they were not priests.
options.note: The text does not diminish their role; it stresses the same fair process applied to them.

options.text: Only the family heads cast lots, while younger brothers were left out.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly includes the family heads and their younger brothers alike in the casting of lots.

quiz.q: What does this chapter's fair use of the lot ultimately teach about God?

options.text: That God is impartial and orderly, giving every family, whatever its size or status, its rightful place in His service.
options.note: The lot, understood in Proverbs 16:33 as belonging finally to the LORD, decides fairly between families great and small.

options.text: That God is indifferent to how His house is organized.
options.note: The opposite; the careful, repeated attention to fair process shows God cares deeply about order in worship.

options.text: That only the largest families deserve significant roles.
options.note: The lot deliberately overrides mere size advantage in determining each specific course.

options.text: That human leaders like David have no real role in ordering worship.
options.note: David actively organizes the process; he simply submits the specific assignments to the lot rather than his own preference.

questions: Nadab and Abihu's judgment could have ended the priesthood, yet God preserved and multiplied it through Eleazar and Ithamar. Where have you seen God bring a fresh start after failure rather than letting it be the final word?

The lot gave every priestly family, large or small, an equal and fair place in serving God's house. Where might you be tempted to think your place or task in serving God matters less than someone else's more visible role?

A priest of the eighth course, Abijah, was simply doing his ordinary, rotating duty when an angel announced the coming of Christ's forerunner. How does this encourage you to keep faithfully doing the ordinary tasks God has given you?
This whole system of rotating priests existed because no single priest could serve forever. How does knowing that Jesus is a permanent, unending priest change the way you approach Him today?

The returning exiles needed this kind of careful record to reestablish true worship after Babylon. What unglamorous, detailed faithfulness is God asking of you right now in order to rebuild or sustain something good?

translationNotes.note: The BSB renders a long descriptive clause about the recording of names as a single sentence; the original source's em dash marking the alternating family pattern was rendered here as a comma for a clean break.

The phrase 'the family heads and their younger brothers alike' translates a construction stressing that no distinction in status affected the casting of lots; a source em dash was converted to a comma.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 24:1-31. Source em dashes at verses 6 and 31 were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
